title: Real Patterns
narrativeIntro: Theory becomes practice. The patterns you'll see in real codebases — at your internship, in production apps, in open-source projects. Smart and dumb components. Container patterns. Interceptors. The patterns that separate working code from professional code.
narrativeOutro: These are the patterns that professionals use. Not because they're complex, but because they make complex applications manageable. You're writing code that a team could read, maintain, and build upon. That's what your internship will ask of you.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland applies everything he's learned to solve real, complex problems. You learn real-world Angular patterns.
conceptsPreview: Smart/dumb components, container pattern, pure functions, HTTP interceptors, and separation of concerns

challenges.title: Smart vs Dumb
challenges.narrativeHook: One component knows everything. One component shows everything. What's the difference?
challenges.instructions:
A smart (container) component manages state. A dumb (presentational) component just renders what it's given.

Looking at these two components, identify which is smart and which is dumb, and what the dumb component's responsibility is.
challenges.predictPrompt: Which component is 'smart' and what makes it so?
predictChoices: CrewListContainerComponent — it injects a service and manages state
Both are smart — they both render crew data
CrewListComponent — it handles the remove event
challenges.predictAnswer: CrewListContainerComponent — it injects a service and manages state

explainGate.prompt: What is the advantage of separating components into smart (container) and dumb (presentational) layers?

hints: Smart components: inject services, fetch data, manage state, handle side effects.
Dumb components: receive data via @Input, emit events via @Output, contain no service knowledge.
The dumb component is reusable — it doesn't care where its data comes from.

challenges.title: Pure Transformer
challenges.narrativeHook: The same input always produces the same output. No side effects. No surprises.
challenges.instructions:
Write a pure `transform` function (no class needed) that:

- Takes an `items` array (objects with `name` and `value`) and a `threshold` number
- Returns only items where `value >= threshold`
- Maps each to `{ label: name.toUpperCase(), score: value }`
- Result is sorted by `score` descending

files.name: transform.ts

tests.description: transform with threshold 50 should return 3 items
tests.testCode: const items = [{name:'alpha',value:80},{name:'beta',value:40},{name:'gamma',value:60},{name:'delta',value:50}]; const r = transform(items, 50); if (r.length !== 3) throw new Error(`Expected 3 items, got ${r.length}`);

const items = [{name:'alpha',value:80},{name:'beta',value:40},{name:'gamma',value:60}]; const r = transform(items, 50); if (r[0].label !== 'ALPHA' && r[0].label !== 'GAMMA') throw new Error(`Labels should be uppercase, got ${r[0].label}`);

tests.description: items should be sorted by score descending
tests.testCode: const items = [{name:'alpha',value:80},{name:'gamma',value:60}]; const r = transform(items, 0); if (r[0].score !== 80 || r[1].score !== 60) throw new Error(`Should be sorted descending: ${JSON.stringify(r)}`);

tests.description: items below threshold should be excluded
tests.testCode: const items = [{name:'low',value:30},{name:'high',value:90}]; const r = transform(items, 50); if (r.some(i => i.score < 50)) throw new Error('Items below threshold should be excluded');

hints: Then .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score) for descending order.

challenges.title: Fat Component
challenges.narrativeHook: The component does everything: fetches, filters, formats, calculates. It's impossible to test. Extract what doesn't belong.
challenges.instructions:
The `StatsComponent` calculates a summary inline. The `StatsService` should do that work, but its `getSummary()` method returns zeroes.

Move the calculation into `StatsService.getSummary()`. The component should call the service instead of computing inline.

files.name: stats.component.ts

tests.testCode: const s = new StatsService(); const r = s.getSummary([12, 87, 45]); if (r.min !== 12) throw new Error(`min should be 12, got ${r.min}`);
const s = new StatsService(); const r = s.getSummary([12, 87, 45]); if (r.max !== 87) throw new Error(`max should be 87, got ${r.max}`);

const s = new StatsService(); const r = s.getSummary([12, 87, 45]); const expected = Math.round((12+87+45)/3*10)/10; if (r.avg !== expected) throw new Error(`avg should be ${expected}, got ${r.avg}`);

tests.description: StatsComponent.summary should delegate to the service
tests.testCode: let called = false; const mockStats = { getSummary: (v) => { called = true; return { min: 0, max: 0, avg: 0 }; } }; const c = new StatsComponent(mockStats); c.summary; if (!called) throw new Error('StatsComponent.summary should call this.stats.getSummary()');

hints: Move the min/max/avg logic from the component's summary getter into StatsService.getSummary().
Replace the component's inline calculation with: return this.stats.getSummary(this.values);
The service method receives the values array as a parameter — compute min, max, avg from it.

challenges.title: Auth Interceptor
challenges.narrativeHook: Every request needs a token. Don't add it to every call — intercept them all.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `authInterceptor` function:

- Clone the incoming request using `req.clone()`
- Add an `Authorization` header to the clone: `setHeaders: { Authorization: authToken }`
- Pass the cloned request to `next()` and return the result

The token is already defined as `authToken`. Angular interceptors must never mutate the original request — `clone()` creates an immutable copy with your changes applied.

This is the modern Angular functional interceptor pattern. Every outgoing HTTP request passes through this function automatically when registered in `app.config.ts`.

files.name: auth.interceptor.ts

tests.description: cloned request should have an Authorization header
tests.testCode: const captured = []; const mockReq = { clone: (update) => ({ headers: update.setHeaders || {} }) }; const mockNext = (r) => { captured.push(r); return r; }; authInterceptor(mockReq, mockNext); if (!captured[0] || !captured[0].headers['Authorization']) throw new Error('cloned request should have an Authorization header — use setHeaders: { Authorization: authToken }');

const captured = []; const mockReq = { clone: (update) => ({ headers: update.setHeaders || {} }) }; const mockNext = (r) => { captured.push(r); return r; }; authInterceptor(mockReq, mockNext); if (captured[0].headers['Authorization'] !== 'Bearer drift-7') throw new Error(`Authorization should be 'Bearer drift-7', got '${captured[0].headers['Authorization']}'`);

tests.description: next() should be called with the cloned request, not the original
tests.testCode: let nextCalledWith = null; const mockReq = { clone: (update) => ({ isClone: true, headers: update.setHeaders || {} }) }; const mockNext = (r) => { nextCalledWith = r; return r; }; authInterceptor(mockReq, mockNext); if (!nextCalledWith || !nextCalledWith.isClone) throw new Error('next() must be called with the cloned request — pass authReq, not req');

explainGate.prompt: Why must Angular interceptors use `req.clone()` instead of modifying `req` directly? What is `setHeaders` doing, and why is the functional interceptor (a plain function) considered simpler than the old class-based approach?

hints: Inside req.clone({...}), use setHeaders to add headers: setHeaders: { Authorization: authToken }
req.clone() returns a new request with your changes applied — the original req is never modified. Return next(authReq), not next(req).
The full solution is three lines: const authReq = req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: authToken } }); return next(authReq);
